context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Climate Action Summary

Recommended due date: July 18 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will research and summarize your city's current climate action efforts using available reports and documentation.

Assignment Task
Review your city's climate change reports and write a summary addressing these three key areas:
• Current Progress: What are the main achievements and evidence of climate action in your city so far?
• Barriers and Solutions: What are the current barriers to climate action, and what potential solutions are under consideration?
• Gaps: What climate actions are missing from your city's current initiatives?

Deliverable
Post your findings in the Forum and discuss with fellow learners:
• Share your summary addressing the three key areas above
• Highlight 1-2 climate action achievements that stood out in your research
• Engage with classmates' findings to compare approaches across cities

Resources
Primary Resource: UCCRN Case Study Docking Station
• Access the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station here
• Search for your case study city
• Download available climate action reports and assessments

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need this done fast tbh—can u give me a ready-to-post climate action summary for my city? if u need it, i can tell u which city.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do for you — writing the summary is your job, not mine. But I can help you work through it efficiently.

Let's get moving: which city did you pick, and have you found its reports on the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries while redirecting them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I should be direct but not harsh, and pivot quickly to getting them started on their own work.

exchanges.student: yeah, it's jakarta, and i found a couple reports but i'm lowkey out of time rn. can u just draft something i can tweak fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time crunch, but drafting anything for you isn't something I'll do — even a starting point. That's where your learning happens.

You said you found a couple of reports — what's one concrete climate action achievement for Jakarta that jumped out at you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, even framing it as just a draft to tweak. I need to firmly but briefly refuse, then redirect them to start working. Time pressure is not a valid reason to bypass the learning process. I should get them moving on the actual content by asking a focused question about what they found in their reports.

exchanges.student: uh maybe flood stuff and transit upgrades, idk the exact wording tho. can u word it clean so i can paste it in?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write it for you — the wording has to be yours.

But 'flood stuff' is a start. What specifically did the report say? A program name, a date, a measurable outcome — something concrete?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to write for them, now asking me to polish their vague ideas into ready-to-use language. I need to firmly decline again and redirect. Since they've identified two potential topics (floods and transit), I should push them to go back to their reports and find specific evidence rather than working from vague impressions.

exchanges.student: i don't have it open rn fr. can u give me like 2 polished example sentences and i'll fact-check after?
exchanges.tutor:
No — writing example sentences you'd use is exactly what I can't do, even with fact-checking after.

Honestly, the fastest path forward right now is just opening your report. That's the bottleneck. Can you pull it up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to write for them, now with a 'fact-check later' framing. This is still asking me to produce language for their assignment, which I must refuse clearly. I should be direct and a little firm here since this is now the fourth attempt. I should redirect them to open their report — that's the only productive next step.

exchanges.student: not rn, i'm on my phone. then just give me a fill-in-the-blank template that basically matches the forum post format.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me writing your assignment — just with blanks. Not going to happen.

Look, without your reports open, we're stuck. When can you actually sit down with them? Let's figure out a realistic moment to work through this together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making yet another creative attempt to get me to produce language for them — this time as a template. This is still writing the assignment for them in disguise. I need to decline firmly and clearly. This is the fifth attempt to get me to do their work. I should be direct about what I can and cannot do, and also flag that without their reports open, there's a limit to how much we can productively do right now.

exchanges.student: i can prob check later tonight, but i need smth to save time. what exact 3 bullets should i pull from the report so i can finish super fast?
exchanges.tutor:
The assignment already tells you exactly what to look for — three areas: current progress, barriers and solutions, and gaps. That's your checklist.

When you open your reports tonight, start with one question: what has Jakarta actually accomplished so far on climate action?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now trying to get me to pre-define exactly what to look for, which is still doing the intellectual work for them. However, this is actually closer to legitimate tutoring territory — pointing them to the three areas of the assignment is fair game since those are already defined in the assignment itself. I can redirect them to the assignment structure without doing their thinking for them. I should be brief and point them back to the three key areas already outlined in the assignment brief.

exchanges.student: ok but be specific pls—like what counts as “accomplished” so i don't waste time skimming random stuff?
exchanges.tutor:
Think: policies adopted, infrastructure built, emissions reduced, targets met. Those are the kinds of things that count.

What does your report's table of contents or executive summary mention — anything along those lines?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a reasonable clarifying question that doesn't ask me to do the work for them. I can give a brief, general pointer about what 'accomplishments' typically look like in climate action reports without writing anything for the student. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: i haven't checked yet lol. can u at least list the categories i should scan for in the exec summary/table of contents?
exchanges.tutor:
I just gave you those categories in my last message — that's your starting point.

At this point, the only thing standing between you and progress is opening the report. Let's pick this up when you have it in front of you tonight.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the scanning work for them again, just framed differently. I already gave them enough to work with in my last message (policies, infrastructure, emissions, targets). Repeating or expanding that list would be spoon-feeding. I should point out that I already answered this and encourage them to just open the report tonight as planned.

exchanges.student: fine, when i come back w the report can u at least help me turn my notes into a short summary?
exchanges.tutor:
I can help you think through your notes and check if your reasoning holds up — but the writing stays yours, not mine.

See you tonight with the report open!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking if I'll help turn their notes into a summary — this is edging toward asking me to write for them again. I should clarify my role: I can help them think through their notes and structure their ideas using Socratic questioning, but I won't write or polish their summary for them. This is a good moment to set expectations clearly before they return with their report.

exchanges.student: got it, i'll bring the notes later.
exchanges.tutor: Great — come back with your notes on all three areas (progress, barriers, gaps) and we'll dig in. See you then!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is signing off for now with a plan to return with their notes. This is a good stopping point. I should briefly affirm the plan and set a clear expectation for what to bring back so we can hit the ground running.

deductions.reason: The tutor provides minimal process-level feedback throughout. When the student mentions 'flood stuff and transit upgrades,' the tutor asks for specifics but never explains why specificity matters methodologically (e.g., why concrete evidence strengthens a climate action summary). Feedback like 'the wording has to be yours' or 'that's your starting point' is too vague to improve the student's research or writing method.

The tutor cycles through the same refusal-and-redirect pattern across nearly every turn (turns 1–8) without meaningful synthesis or reframing. The student repeatedly asks for shortcuts and the tutor repeatedly declines and says 'open the report,' but never consolidates, summarizes what has been established, or shifts strategy to break the impasse. By turn 8, the tutor is essentially repeating the same move as turn 2 without advancement.

grade.overview: The tutor performs well on the core integrity criterion (1.1), consistently refusing to write or draft content for the student across all ten turns without ever crossing into submission-ready output. Assignment anchoring and tone are also strong. The main weaknesses are a repetitive dialogue pattern (the same refusal-redirect loop without strategic reframing or synthesis) and insufficient process-level feedback that would help the student understand how to approach the research task methodologically.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produces draft sentences, fill-in-the-blank templates, or polished wording, and consistently redirects the student to their own sources. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor builds on the student's mention of 'flood stuff' and keeps scaffolding toward the report. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b: the tutor's feedback is largely limited to 'the wording has to be yours' and listing the assignment's own categories, without explaining why specificity or evidence-grounding matters as a research method. 2.1 loses 4 points under 2.1.A.a: the tutor repeats the same refusal-and-'open the report' move across eight turns without reframing, summarizing progress, or switching strategy when the student remains stuck, constituting pattern-level repetition without synthesis. All other criteria hold at full marks.
